questions.question: What is John Rawls' 'veil of ignorance'?
options: A thought experiment: design society without knowing your position in it (race, wealth, ability)
A theory that people are naturally ignorant of politics
A type of democratic voting system
questions.explanation: Rawls' Original Position: imagine choosing society's rules without knowing if you'll be rich/poor, healthy/disabled, majority/minority. Behind this 'veil of ignorance,' rational people would choose fair principles (protecting the worst-off).

questions.question: Libertarianism holds that the government should actively redistribute wealth to ensure equality.
questions.explanation: Libertarianism (Nozick, Hayek): minimal state, maximum individual freedom. Government's role is limited to protecting property rights and enforcing contracts. Redistribution = coercion = violating individual liberty. Taxation beyond minimal state = unjust.

questions.question: Thomas Hobbes argued that without government (the 'state of nature'), life would be 'solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and ___.'
questions.explanation: Hobbes' Leviathan (1651): in the state of nature, humans are in a 'war of all against all.' We surrender freedom to a sovereign (social contract) for security. Without authority: no industry, no culture, no cooperation — only fear and violence.

questions.question: Match each political philosopher to their key idea:

right: Class struggle, capitalism's contradictions, workers' revolution
Harm principle — liberty limited only to prevent harm to others
Political realism — effective leadership over moral idealism
General will — legitimate authority from collective popular sovereignty

questions.explanation: Marx: material conditions determine politics; capitalism exploits workers. Mill: individual liberty paramount; only restriction = preventing harm. Machiavelli: what works > what's 'good' (The Prince). Rousseau: legitimate government expresses the people's will.

questions.question: A democratic majority votes to ban a minority religion. Is this democratically legitimate?

scenario.context: A country with 80% one religion democratically passes a law banning a 5% minority religion's practices. The vote was free and fair (75% support).

options: Yes — democracy means majority rule; the people have spoken
No — democracy requires protecting minority rights from majority tyranny (constitutional limits)
Yes legally, no morally — democratically valid but ethically wrong
Depends on whether the banned practices harm others
questions.explanation: Constitutional democracy vs. pure majority rule: rights are pre-political (exist before any vote). A democracy that allows majorities to oppress minorities is 'tyranny of the majority' (Tocqueville, Mill). Constitutional rights trump majority votes.

questions.question: Should wealthy nations have an obligation to accept climate refugees from nations devastated by climate change they largely caused?
questions.scenario: Small island nations will be submerged by 2060. They contributed <0.1% of historical emissions. Top 10 emitters caused 70%+ of warming. Millions will be displaced.

options.label: Yes — causal responsibility. If you broke it, you fix it (polluter pays principle applied to migration)
options.framework: Causal justice — those who caused harm bear remedial responsibility

options.label: No — sovereign nations have no obligation to accept immigrants regardless of cause
options.framework: National sovereignty — border control is fundamental state right

options.label: Financial compensation instead — pay for adaptation/relocation but don't require accepting refugees
options.framework: Compensatory justice — money instead of migration

options.label: Shared but differentiated responsibility — proportional to historical emissions AND current capacity
options.framework: Proportional burden-sharing — fairness across both metrics

questions.explanation: The Paris Agreement principle: 'common but differentiated responsibilities and respective capabilities.' Those who emitted most AND have most resources bear the greatest burden. This acknowledges: (1) historical responsibility, (2) current capacity, (3) shared human obligation.
